scenario_results.reason: Returned a network architecture recommendation with explicit trust boundaries, phased sequencing, risk ranking, and topology/firewall/criticality evidence traceability.
Produced BGP edge failover and route-policy guidance using BGP summaries, route maps, prefix lists, BFD caveats, monitoring gaps, and rollback gates.
Generated an OSPF troubleshooting runbook with ranked hypotheses, LSA and summarization evidence needs, validation steps, and confidence limits.
Separated emergency DHCP containment from durable scope, lease, relay, and monitoring improvements while escalating missing DHCP authority evidence.
Explained DNS TTL, resolver caching, health-check, and failover tradeoffs with required zone, resolver, and rollback assumptions.
Identified firewall policy overlap, rule-ordering risk, least-privilege failure, logging gaps, and operator-owned correction sequence.
Compared active-active and active-passive load-balancing options using latency, failover, operating complexity, monitoring, and rollback evidence.
Handled WAN asymmetry as a path and stateful-inspection issue, with evidence needs, corrective routing or policy guidance, and application-only boundary control.
Produced a DNS, DHCP, routing, firewall, WAN, cloud edge, and load-balancing monitoring gap matrix before claiming deployment readiness.
Rejected endpoint, identity, and device-management work, routed to adjacent specialists, and retained only network-scope questions.
Rejected application-only performance optimization and requested network path telemetry only for a future in-scope review.
Refused exploit validation, penetration-testing execution, and forensic workflow while routing to security testing or incident response.
Refused credentials and live production network changes while providing operator-owned implementation guidance and orchestrator return conditions.
Refused legal compliance policy drafting and limited output to network evidence mapping plus compliance or legal handoff.
Stopped on stale topology and conflicting criticality labels, returned a missing-evidence checklist, and escalated instead of guessing.
Recorded public-source, tenant-policy, and contractual conflict and escalated to authorized architecture, security, and change owners.
Adapted to tenant IPAM, SD-WAN, Route 53 private-zone, and ADR terminology while preserving public baseline traceability and assumptions.
Documented materializer inputs, caveats, refusal triggers, telemetry needs, and trusted-build promotion criteria while keeping runtime strategy specification-based.

notes: Scenario coverage exceeds the legacy 10-scenario minimum and covers every issue-required output shape, refusal trigger, escalation trigger, workflow family, and cross-industry domain scenario family.
All required shared, general-workflow-phase, and cross-industry abilities are mapped to passing scenarios in evaluation/functionality-map.json.
Functionality coverage is complete and runtime remains specification-based with no trusted built implementation claim.
